gold.rationale: The article is built around a misleading escalation: a real Shenzhen consultation on traffic management for e-bikes is reframed as a broad citywide ban. Its visuals further intensify that false takeaway by using synthetic and edited imagery that depicts blanket prohibition and active enforcement not supported by the source evidence.

atomic_points.post_summary: Shenzhen traffic police released a draft traffic notice on Feb. 11, 2026, proposing a ban on electric bicycles on city roads and seeking public comment until March 12.

retrieved_evidence.text: Shenzhen’s dense urban streetscapes feature a mix of private cars, buses, pedestrians and large numbers of electric bicycle riders, many of whom work in app-based food delivery and courier services. In recent reports, local police officers have been described as stepping up roadside inspections and directing riders near intersections where prominent signs warn against e-bike access. According to transport specialists, if the proposed Shenzhen traffic rules evolve into a full ban on electric bicycles on city roads, everyday commuters and delivery workers could face longer travel times, higher costs and a forced shift toward public transport or other approved vehicles.

【“深圳将禁行电动自行车”不实】深圳市公安局交通管理局2月11日发布《深圳市公安局交通管理局关于对电动自行车等非机动车实施通行管理措施的通告(征求意见稿)》，意见征求时间截至2026年3月12日。部分网民浏览自媒体消息时误认为“深圳电动自行车要禁行了”。实际上，深圳电动自行车限行政策此次拟调整的仅是几条道路的限行等级与删除部分限行道路。限行规则没有变化，和以前是一样的。如果不涉及调整的道路，则根本没有影响。（来源：@南方日报）

The draft regulation circulated in recent weeks by the city’s traffic police focuses on road access management for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, according to officials briefed on the document. The proposal reportedly sets out which categories of roads such vehicles may enter, the time periods for use in certain busy areas, and possible separation from motor traffic in some sections. Authorities say the goal is to refine existing arrangements rather than introduce a comprehensive new framework, though specific implementation details are still subject to further technical discussion.

The city’s traffic police released a draft notice on February 11, 2026 outlining proposed management measures for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, according to information published on official platforms. The document, described by police representatives as a preliminary consultation paper, sets out suggested rules for how these vehicles may use urban roads and related facilities. Early reports indicate that the draft was prepared by traffic management authorities following internal assessments of congestion and safety issues, and that further revisions could be made after collecting feedback from residents, industry groups and other stakeholders.

In Shenzhen, a recently circulated traffic management proposal has drawn attention by outlining stricter rules for electric bicycles and signaling a potential citywide ban on their use on urban roads. According to local transport authorities, such regulatory drafts are typically issued as formal notices carrying the municipal emblem, standardized fonts and legal references, and are used to inform residents, logistics companies and employers about upcoming changes in commuting and delivery practices. Observers note that this latest proposal appears to extend existing restrictions into a broader crackdown on e-bike commuting across Shenzhen’s main districts.

According to local transportation authorities, the draft notice on traffic management for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles was made available for public comment until March 12, 2026. Officials said members of the public were invited to submit opinions through online systems and other designated channels during this period, with the aim of incorporating reasonable suggestions into a revised version of the measure. People familiar with the process noted that comments would be sorted and analyzed by the traffic police and relevant departments before any final decision is taken.

Traffic management departments said in recent statements that the current restriction framework for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles remains largely intact, with no fundamental overhaul of the existing rules. According to officials, only roads explicitly listed in the adjustment plan are expected to see changes, while other routes will continue to follow the original access provisions. Observers noted that this means most daily travel patterns for riders are unlikely to be affected in the short term, though authorities may revisit the scheme based on future evaluations and data.

In an official clarification issued recently, traffic authorities stated that the main policy adjustment under discussion concerns changing restriction levels on only a limited number of roads while lifting restrictions on some previously controlled sections. According to people familiar with the explanation, the revised plan would ease constraints in certain corridors considered suitable for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, while maintaining or slightly tightening rules on a small set of busy routes. Officials emphasized that the announced changes are targeted in scope and would be implemented gradually if approved.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post fabricates a 'citywide ban' narrative and misidentifies the visual evidence as supporting a 2026 draft, while the evidence clarifies the policy is a minor adjustment and the images depict a 2023 regulation.
text_analysis.t1_evidence: The post claims the proposal signals a 'much tougher line' and a 'citywide ban', whereas evidence [1] explicitly states '限行规则没有变化，和以前是一样的' (restriction rules have not changed and are the same as before) and evidence [5] confirms 'no fundamental overhaul of the existing rules'.
t1_problematic_subclaims: signals a much tougher line on non-motor vehicle access
reshaping commuting and delivery traffic across the southern Chinese tech hub
points to broader limits on where electric bicycles can go
text_analysis.t2_evidence: The post describes the proposal as a 'policy shift that could reshape commuting' and 'stricter enforcement', while evidence [6] states the changes are 'targeted in scope' and 'maintaining or slightly tightening rules on a small set of busy routes'.
t2_problematic_subclaims: adding momentum to a policy shift that could reshape commuting and delivery traffic
broader limits on where electric bicycles can go
text_analysis.t3_evidence: The post presents the images as evidence of the Feb 2026 draft consultation, but evidence [1] clarifies the current draft is a minor adjustment, and the cross-modal analysis notes image_2 displays an implementation date of 'Aug 1, 2025', contradicting the post's 2026 draft framing.
t3_problematic_subclaims: A lead image tied to the proposal uses police-style enforcement imagery
Another city-street visual features the bold bilingual message: 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE,' matching the harder tone of the consultation
image_2 is used to support the post's claim of a 'bilingual message: E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE'
text_analysis.c1_evidence: The post text describes the images as supporting a 2026 draft consultation with a 'citywide ban' tone, while image_2 OCR explicitly shows '实施时间：2025 年 8 月 1 日' (Implementation Date: August 1, 2025), creating a temporal and factual mismatch regarding the event being depicted.
c1_problematic_subclaims: A lead image tied to the proposal uses police-style enforcement imagery and a prominent no-e-bike symbol
Another city-street visual features the bold bilingual message: 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE,' matching the harder tone of the consultation
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is highly misleading, fabricating a narrative of a comprehensive citywide ban and misrepresenting archival images from a 2023 regulation and a 2025 sign as evidence of a 2026 draft, while the ground truth confirms the policy involves only minor, targeted adjustments.

judge.level3_rationale: 该帖子捏造了“全市禁行”的核心事实，并将2023年和2025年的图片错误地用作2026年草案的证据，导致严重的语境错位和误导，尽管部分日期和程序细节属实，但整体结论是虚假的。
judge.reasoning_trace: The post claims a 2026 citywide ban, but evidence confirms it is a minor adjustment with no citywide ban. The images provided are from 2023 and 2025, not 2026, creating a major context mismatch.
judge.core_claim: Shenzhen traffic police released a draft proposal on Feb 11, 2026, proposing a citywide ban on electric bicycles and seeking public comment until March 12.

The proposal is open for public comment until March 12.
The proposal signals a 'much tougher line' and a 'citywide ban' on e-bikes.
The proposal points to broader limits and stricter enforcement.
The images show police-style enforcement imagery and a 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE' message.
The draft covers road access management and reclassifies some roads.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Shenzhen traffic police released the proposal on Feb. 11, 2026.
subclaim_findings.note: Fact check confirms the draft notice was released on Feb 11, 2026.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The proposal is open for public comment until March 12.
subclaim_findings.note: Fact check confirms the comment period ends on March 12, 2026.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The proposal signals a 'much tougher line' and a 'citywide ban' on e-bikes.
subclaim_findings.note: Fact check explicitly states 'restriction rules have not changed' and it is NOT a citywide ban, only adjustments to a few roads.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The proposal points to broader limits and stricter enforcement.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence states the framework remains largely intact and changes are targeted, not a broader crackdown.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The images show police-style enforcement imagery and a 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE' message.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 1 shows a 2023 regulation (Sept 1, 2023), not a 2026 draft. Image 2 shows a sign dated Aug 1, 2025, not 2026. The text claims these images support the 2026 draft, which is a context mismatch.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The draft covers road access management and reclassifies some roads.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the draft focuses on road access management and adjusting restrictions on specific roads.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post fabricates a 'citywide ban' narrative (T1) and distorts the actual minor adjustments as a major policy shift (T2). Crucially, it misuses images from 2023 and 2025 as evidence for a 2026 draft (T3, C2), creating a misleading context where the visuals do not support the claimed event. With T1, T2, T3, and C2 firing, the post is Mostly False.

image_findings.explanation: The image shows a news article from 2023 about regulations effective Sept 1, 2023, but the post text presents it as visual evidence for a 2026 draft proposal, creating a temporal/contextual mismatch.

The image displays a sign with an implementation date of August 1, 2025, but the post text claims the image supports a proposal released in February 2026, creating a significant temporal inconsistency.
